cves.description:
evernote-mcp-server openBrowser Command Injection Privilege Escalation Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows local attackers to escalate privileges on affected installations of evernote-mcp-server. An attacker must first obtain the ability to execute low-privileged code on the target system in order to exploit this vulnerability.

The specific flaw exists within the openBrowser function. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of a user-supplied string before using it to execute
cves.how_introduced: This commit created auth.js and introduced the entire vulnerable flow: openBrowser(url), the exec(`${command} "${url}"`) shell invocation, and redirectToAuthorization() building authUrl from requestToken. The fix commit rewrites exactly that function, and blame on the pre-fix file assigns the exec line, the function body, and the authUrl construction/openBrowser call back to e08547bc. Pickaxe searches for function openBrowser(url), the exec import, and openBrowser(authUrl) all trace their first appearance to this commit.

bug_commits.author: brentmid
bug_commits.message: Add comprehensive OAuth 1.0a authentication and testing suite

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the entire initial implementation of `auth.js`, including the use of `child_process.exec` to open the system browser. This specific implementation used template literals to concatenate a user-controlled URL directly into a shell command, which is the root cause of the command injection vulnerability fixed in the subsequent commit.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: 1. Blamed commit e08547 introduces auth.js and the child_process.exec dependency. 2. It implements the openBrowser function using shell execution: exec(`${command} "${url}"`). 3. This pattern allows an attacker to break out of the URL string and execute arbitrary system commands via the shell.

cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function sort_cmp of the file src/array.c. Such manipulation leads to use after free. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The name of the patch is eb398971bfb43c38db3e04528b68ac9a7ce509bc. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: This commit refactored `sort_cmp()` to compare copied element values instead of indices and, in the process, replaced the earlier mutation guard `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p || size < a || size < b` with the weaker pointer-only check `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`. The fix commit `eb398971` changes exactly that logic by snapshotting both the array pointer and length and validating both after user code runs. Even though the sort implementation was already unsafe earlier, this was a reimplementation/regression of the vulnerable logic and therefore a valid BIC.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: array: implement hybrid sorting with algorithm selection

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `sort_cmp` function signature and logic that relied on the pointer `p` to detect array modifications. By storing the pointer `p` as the base address of the array at the start of the sort and only checking `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p` later, it failed to account for scenarios where `realloc` changes the buffer size without changing the base pointer, or where the length of the array changes in a way that invalidates the previously captured pointer `p` before the check occurs.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced a comparison mechanism in `sort_cmp` that captures a pointer `p` (RARRAY_PTR(ary)) to detect array reallocations. However, this check was insufficient to detect all forms of reallocations or modifications (specifically those changing length or buffer content without necessarily changing the head pointer), allowing the heapify or insertion sort logic to continue using stale pointers into a reallocated buffer, leading to use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit refactored `sort_cmp()` to compare copied element values instead of indices and, in the process, replaced the earlier mutation guard `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p || size < a || size < b` with the weaker pointer-only check `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`. The fix commit `eb398971` changes exactly that logic by snapshotting both the array pointer and length and validating both after user code runs. Even though the sort implementation was already unsafe earlier, this was a reimplementation/regression of the vulnerable logic and therefore a valid BIC.

cves.description: vet MCP Server SSE Transport DNS Rebinding Vulnerability
cves.how_introduced: This commit added `sseHandlerWithHeadSupport` and hard-coded `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: "*"` for `/sse` HEAD requests, then served the wrapped handler via `http.Server.ListenAndServe()`. The fix directly rewrites those HEAD-response lines and places the handler behind the new `originGuard` and `hostGuard`, so cd7caff authored vulnerable code that was part of the CVE fix. It was not the original omission of host/origin validation, but it did add a new insecure code path to the same SSE transport.

This established the insecure handling pattern that allowed the DNS rebinding vulnerability, which was subsequently mitigated in the fix commit by introducing `originGuard` and `hostGuard` wrappers.

screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of Host header verification or origin checking in SSE transport connections, allowing unauthorized requests to bypass same-origin protections.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit added an HTTP handler that accepted SSE requests without validating the origin or Host header, and specifically included a permissive wildcard CORS policy (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *), thereby creating the vulnerable surface area.

cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the exact optimization that the fix changes: it added the 'invert JMPNOT to JMPIF' block and the write `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` without first verifying that the linked jump was actually an `OP_JMPNOT`. The fix commit e50f15c1 directly patches this code by adding `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT` to the condition. Pre-2b72 code always emitted a normal `JMP` around failure handling and never rewrote prior instructions, so the bytecode-corruption bug starts here.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization logic which lacked validation to ensure the instruction being replaced was actually an OP_JMPNOT. The fix subsequently added this missing check (`s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`), confirming that the original implementation allowed incorrect instructions to be overwritten, leading to the bytecode corruption.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. Without this check, other instruction types (like JMP from undefined variables) could be incorrectly overwritten with `OP_JMPIF`, corrupting the bytecode execution and triggering the use-after-free.

The fix commit e50f15c1 directly patches this code by adding `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT` to the condition. Pre-2b72 code always emitted a normal `JMP` around failure handling and never rewrote prior instructions, so the bytecode-corruption bug starts here.

cves.description:
A flaw was found in mirror-registry where an authenticated user can trick the system into accessing unintended internal or restricted systems by providing malicious web addresses. 

When the application processes these addresses, it automatically follows redirects without verifying the final destination, allowing attackers to route requests to systems they should not have access to.
cves.how_introduced: This commit made the feature actively vulnerable by adding the Harbor/Quay adapters, the registry-adapter factory, the verify/repository-discovery API logic, and worker discovery code that performed outbound HTTP to the stored URL. Blame on the pre-fix code shows the `self.session.get(...)` calls in both adapters were authored here and used requests' default redirect-following behavior, while `RegistryAdapter` also lacked any destination validation. The fix directly patches these exact additions by validating URLs in the adapter constructor and disabling redirects (`allow_redirects=False`), so this is a confirmed introducer of the exploitable redirect-based SSRF path.

cves.description: eBay API MCP Server Affected by Environment Variable Injection
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the entire `updateEnvFile()` helper in `src/auth/oauth.ts` and added the call sites that persist refreshed/user/app tokens into `.env`. The helper serialized values with raw string interpolation (`const newLine = `${key}=${value}``) and regex-based replacement, with no escaping for quotes/newlines or safe dotenv serialization, which is exactly the behavior the fix replaces with `dotenv.parse()` + `dotenv-stringify()`. Pickaxe/history checks show this is the first commit that added `.env` writeback logic at all, so it is the original bug-introducing commit for the environment-variable injection flaw.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the shard-doubling and shard-retry logic in `RateLimiter`, including `_is_wcu_exhausted()` and the `old_buckets`-based decision path. The fix replaces that inference with an explicit `SpeculativeFailureReason` from the repository and only retries on `APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`, showing that 9f66's original recovery logic was the flawed behavior. In particular, using raw bucket token state to infer why the conditional write failed made the hot-partition mitigation nondeterministic under mixed app-limit/WCU exhaustion.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted` logic and the initial handling of speculative failures related to shard limits, which the vulnerability analysis identifies as flawed. Specifically, this commit added the rudimentary check that failed to account for correct capacity aggregation and proper handling of DynamoDB throttling states, which the fix commit subsequently replaces with more granular `SpeculativeFailureReason` checks.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented `_is_wcu_exhausted` using a simplified logic check (comparing tokens_milli < 1000) that failed to correctly interpret DynamoDB partition throttling states. This logic error meant that when the system encountered high contention or throttling, it incorrectly triggered shard doubling or retries without proper deduplication or capacity validation, ultimately leading to the reported DoS condition.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the shard-doubling and shard-retry logic in `RateLimiter`, including `_is_wcu_exhausted()` and the `old_buckets`-based decision path. The fix replaces that inference with an explicit `SpeculativeFailureReason` from the repository and only retries on `APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`, showing that 9f66's original recovery logic was the flawed behavior. In particular, using raw bucket token state to infer why the conditional write failed made the hot-partition mitigation nondeterministic under mixed app-limit/WCU exhaustion.

That design concentrated all writes for a given entity onto one DynamoDB partition, which is the root hot-partition condition later mitigated by per-shard bucket PKs. So this commit is a real origin point for the CVE, even though blame drift surfaced unrelated lines.

This logic was flawed because it monitored recent consumption deltas rather than actual token levels, failing to correctly account for sustained load, which directly matches the root cause described in the vulnerability analysis.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Generic webhook channel trusts caller-supplied identity fields; allowlist is checked against untrusted payload data
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the generic webhook channel, its config schema, and the factory wiring. In that first implementation, the JSON payload required caller-supplied `sender` and `chat_id`, the allowlist check was `base_config.is_allowed(&payload.sender)`, and the published `InboundMessage` also used `payload.sender`/`payload.chat_id` directly. The fix replaces exactly that design with server-controlled `sender_id`/`chat_id`, `trust_payload_identity=false` by default, and optional signature verification, so 2c9deef is the causal bug-introducing commit.

By relying solely on this single initial resolution/validation step, the code became susceptible to race conditions where the file system state (e.g., symlinks) could change between the initial check and the subsequent read/write operation.

screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Insufficient path canonicalization or validation before file I/O operations.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit added `validate_path_in_workspace` to `resolve_path` but treated the validation as a one-time setup step rather than a security invariant, creating a TOCTOU vulnerability where the validated path could be replaced by a symlink before actual file I/O occurs.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Generic webhook channel trusts caller-supplied identity fields; allowlist is checked against untrusted payload data

cves.description: Babylon Incorrect FP inactive accounting in costaking creates “phantom stake” that earns rewards after BTC unbond
cves.how_introduced: The fix changes x/costaking/keeper/hooks_finality.go so that AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded subtracts sats whenever the FP was active in the previous set, instead of only when it was active in both previous and current sets. cves.description: Termix has a Stored XSS in File Manager leading to Local File Inclusion (LFI) in Electron and Session Hijacking in Browser
cves.how_introduced: This commit is the first one that introduced the vulnerable SVG rendering path in the file manager: `git log -S` traces `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: content }}` back to `a7fa4039`, and the pattern is absent in the prior release (`5cd9de9`). Blame on the pre-fix file viewer points the vulnerable lines to `a7fa4039`, where attacker-controlled SVG content is inserted directly into the DOM. The fix replaces that behavior with a base64 `data:image/svg+xml` URL rendered through ``, confirming `a7fa4039` authored the stored XSS.

cves.description: zeptoclaw has Android device shell blocklist bypass via argument permutation
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced `src/tools/android/actions.rs` and the original `device_shell()` implementation. In that initial version, dangerous `rm` commands were blocked only by literal substring checks for `"rm -rf"` and `"rm -r"`, which is the flawed design the fix replaced with flag-aware parsing; reordered/combined forms such as `rm -fr`, `rm -f -r`, and long-option equivalents were not parsed and could bypass the guard. Git history shows this file/function did not exist earlier, so this is the commit that introduced the vulnerable Android shell behavior.

cves.description: Fickling has Static Analysis Bypass via Incomplete Dangerous Module Blocklist
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the `StackGlobal.run()` validation `if not module.isidentifier() or not attr.isidentifier(): raise ValueError(...)`. That rejects dotted module names such as `multiprocessing.util`, which are exactly the kind of imports the 2026 fix adjusted to `all(m.isidentifier() for m in module.split("."))`. Because `check_safety()` does not wrap analysis in exception handling, this validation made dotted dangerous imports evade or break static analysis, so it is a real introducer for the dotted-module bypass fixed by `eb299b45`.

cves.description: A weakness has been identified in mruby 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function ary_fill_exec of the file mrbgems/mruby-array-ext/src/array.c. Executing a manipulation of the argument start/length can lead to out-of-bounds write. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. This patch is called 93619f06dd378db6766666b30c08978311c7ec94. It is best practice to apply a patch to resolve this issue.
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the vulnerable C helper `ary_fill_exec` and exposed it as the Ruby method `__fill_exec`. In the new code, `ary_fill_exec` accepted `start` and `length` via `mrb_get_args`, then performed `mrb_value *ptr = ARY_PTR(ary) + start;` and wrote `length` elements without first rejecting negative `start`/`length`. The fix commit `93619f06...` adds exactly those missing validations at the top of this function. Before this commit, `Array#fill` was implemented in Ruby with `self[i] = ...`, so this C reimplementation is the point where the out-of-bounds write bug was introduced.

cves.description: Coolify vulnerable to command injection via docker-compose.yaml parameters
cves.how_introduced: This commit did more than tweak Blade help text: it also rewrote `ApplicationDeploymentJob` to auto-inject only `--env-file` into custom Docker Compose build commands. It still did not enforce `-f`, so a user-supplied `docker compose build` could continue to resolve the repository's compose file instead of Coolify's generated one. The later fix replaces this env-file-only logic with `injectDockerComposeFlags(...)`, so this commit reauthored the vulnerable build-command path.

bug_commits.message: fix: inject environment variables into custom Docker Compose build commands

screening_verification.vuln_description: The application allowed unsanitized user-provided parameters to be included in docker-compose commands, enabling an attacker to inject arbitrary shell commands.

cves.description: theshit's Improper Privilege Dropping Allows Local Privilege Escalation via Command Re-execution
cves.how_introduced: The initial commit created `fix_command`/`get_command_output` and introduced the original privileged command re-execution path: `Command::new(&split_command[0]) ... .output()?` with no `setuid`/`setgid` or other credential drop. Pickaxe shows `fn get_command_output(...)` first appears here, and blame on the pre-fix code still attributes the core command construction and environment setup lines to this commit. The later fix adds explicit permission detection and uid/gid dropping before execution, so `ec1f1462` authored the flawed security design from the start.

The pre-fix file state (`eb5b3c3c...`) blames the execution body in `src/fix.rs` lines 146-173 to `0fc1b4f7`, and those are exactly the vulnerable execution lines replaced by the later security fix. cves.description: BunnyPad Vulnerable to Buffer Overflow When Opening Files of Size 20MB or Greater
cves.how_introduced: This commit is the PyQt5-side reimplementation of the same flaw. The fixed `prettyfonts/v11/v11-PyQt5.py::open_file()` now inserts `MAX_SAFE_SIZE` before `f.read()`, and blame on the pre-fix version attributes the vulnerable read body to 4ed0b21c; `git log -S` also shows the exact string `with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8", errors="replace") as f:` was introduced here for PyQt5. Because it rewrote `open_file()` and preserved the unbounded whole-file read, it newly introduced the vulnerable code in the fixed PyQt5 lineage.

bug_commits.author: Garry Strait
bug_commits.message: Potential fix for code scanning alert no. 1: Workflow does not contain permissions

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit modifies a GitHub Actions workflow file (.github/workflows/build.yml), which is infrastructure-related configuration. The vulnerability is a buffer overflow within the application's file processing logic inside the Python source code (prettyfonts/v11/v11-PyQt6.py). There is no functional connection between the CI/CD configuration and the Python code responsible for the reported memory corruption.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application lacks proper bounds checking or resource allocation limits when processing large files (>=20MB), leading to memory corruption or exhaustion during the file parsing process.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked file read or memory allocation logic that fails to validate input file size before processing buffers, allowing files exceeding 20MB to trigger overflows.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix adds a file-size check to `prettyfonts/v11/v11-PyQt6.py::open_file()` before calling `f.read()`, so the vulnerable behavior is the unconditional whole-file read with no size cap. In the pre-fix file, blame attributes the read path (`path = QFileDialog...`, `with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8", errors="replace") as f:`, `self.textedit.setPlainText(f.read())`) to 12d0e719, and `git log -S` shows this exact PyQt6 implementation first appears in this commit. Although the commit message says it rebased/resynced PyQt6, it rewrote the vulnerable function body and reintroduced it without the missing safeguard, so it is a real BIC.

cves.description: quic-go HTTP/3 QPACK Header Expansion DoS
cves.how_introduced: This qpack v0.6 rewrite authored the exact vulnerable forms later fixed in http3/headers.go, http3/server.go, and http3/stream.go: it replaced slice-based parsing with DecodeFunc-based parseHeaders/requestFromHeaders/updateResponseFromHeaders, but still never enforced any limit on the decompressed field-section size. The fix commit directly adds a sizeLimit parameter to those functions and threads MaxHeaderBytes into the server and client call sites. Even though the flaw existed earlier, this commit reimplemented the vulnerable logic and therefore counts as a real bug-introducing commit.

cves.description:
HTTP::Session2 versions before 1.12 for Perl for Perl may generate weak session ids using the rand() function.

The HTTP::Session2 session id generator returns a SHA-1 hash seeded with the built-in rand function, the epoch time, and the PID. The PID will come from a small set of numbers, and the epoch time may be guessed, if it is not leaked from the HTTP Date header. The built-in rand() function is unsuitable for cryptographic usage.

HTTP::Session2 after version 1.02 will attempt to use the /d

Time::HiRes::time()), int(rand(4)), 31)`, and t/09_random.t explicitly tests that fallback. Blame on the vulnerable lines at the pre-fix state resolves past merge 24844b3 to the boundary/root commit f8a08a8, and the file history shows no intervening commit that introduced or rewrote the generator before 9cfde4d7 replaced it with Crypt::SysRandom. The embedded Changes file also indicates this design predates the current git history (introduced in the 1.02/1.06 era), but f8a08a8 is the first attributable git SHA in this repository carrying the vulnerable code.

cves.description: A path traversal vulnerability exists in Zen MCP Server before 9.8.2 that allows authenticated attackers to read arbitrary files on the system. The vulnerability is caused by flawed logic in the is_dangerous_path() validation function that uses exact string matching against a blacklist of system directories. Attackers can bypass these restrictions by accessing subdirectories of blacklisted paths.
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced `is_dangerous_path()` with the exact-match check `return str(resolved) in DANGEROUS_PATHS or resolved.parent == resolved`, which is the logic later fixed by the security patch. Blame on the vulnerable pre-fix lines attributes the core line to 4151c3c3, and `git log -S` shows this is the commit that first added `is_dangerous_path()`. The same commit also changed `resolve_and_validate_path()` from a safer `relative_to(SECURITY_ROOT)` workspace-boundary model to the weaker blacklist-based model, making arbitrary absolute-path access possible so long as the path was not an exact blacklisted directory string.
